explanations.2: This section lists all the pylint rules we're intentionally turning off because we've decided they don't match how we want to code.
explanations.12: Setting persistent to yes means pylint will save what it learned about the code between runs, so it can give smarter feedback next time.
explanations.16: We're loading extra plugins that add more specialized checks beyond pylint's built-in rules.
explanations.31: Setting jobs to 0 tells pylint to automatically figure out how many processor cores it can use to check code faster in parallel.
explanations.35: When suggestion-mode is on, pylint tries to be helpful by guessing what you actually meant instead of just complaining about errors.
explanations.38: This tells pylint that our code is written for Python version 3.13, so it should check for compatibility with that specific version.
explanations.41: The code will fail the check and exit with an error if the quality score drops below 9.50 out of 10.
